coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble pillar[6]arene functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with excellent supramolecular recognition capability and superior electrochemical sensing performance towards TNT
coredata.dc:creator: Ran, Xin
Qu, Qing
Qian, Xingcan
Xie, Wei
Li, Shunling
Li, Lei
Yang, Long
coredata.dc:description: By combining the merits of water-soluble pillar[6]arenes (WP6s) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), a sensitive electrochemical sensing platform was constructed by using the WP6-N-CQD nanocomposites as an electrode material for the detection of trinitrotoluene (TNT). N-CQDs were prepared via microwave route by using urea and citric acid as raw materials. Two kinds of macrocyclic hosts, β-CD and WP6, were incorporated on to the N-CQDs via hydrogen bonding and π−π interactions, respectively. The FTIR, TGA, and XPS characterizitions demonstrated that the β-CD-N-CQD and WP6-N-CQD composites were successfully prepared. The WP6-N-CQD-modified glass carbon electrode (GCE) indicated an enhanced electrochemical signal than the β-CD-N-CQDs/GCE in the reduction of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), which was attributed to the higher supramolecular recognition capability of the WP6 host than that of β-CD towards TNT. The linear ranges of 0.001μM–1.0μM and 1.0μM–20.0μM were obtained by using this electrochemical sensing platform. The detection limit was 0.95nM (S/N=3). The developed electrochemical sensing technique was successfully applied for the detection of TNT in lake water samples. Therefore, WP6-N-CQD nanocomposites are promising for the construction of high performance electrochemical sensors for the detection of explosives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Pillar[n]arene
Macrocycle
Electrochemical sensing
Explosive
Trinitrotoluene

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of β-CD-N-CQDs and WP6-N-CQD composites
Characterization of β-CD-N-CQD and WP6-N-CQD composites
Electrochemical reduction of TNT on the modified electrodes
Analytical performance of the electrochemical sensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of β-CD-N-CQD and WP6-N-CQD nanocomposites used for electrochemical sensing and recognition of TNT.

(A) The TEM image of the synthesized N-CQDs; inset is the size distribution histogram. (B) The Raman spectrum of N-CQDs. (C) The XRD pattern of N-CQDs. (D) The FTIR spectra of N-CQDs, β-CD-N-CQDs, and WP6-N-CQDs. (E) The TGA curves of N-CQDs, β-CD-N-CQDs, and WP6-N-CQDs. (F) The XPS survey spectra of N-CQDs, β-CD-N-CQDs, and WP6-N-CQDs.

The C 1s (A), N 1s (B), and O 1s (C) high-resolution spectra of the N-CQDs; The C 1s (D), N 1s (E), and O 1s (F) high-resolution spectra of β-CD-N-CQDs; The C 1s (G), N 1s (H), and O 1s (I) high-resolution spectra of WP6-N-CQDs.
The lowest energy docked conformations of β-CD/TNT (A is the top view, B is the side view) and WP6/TNT (C is the top view, D is the side view) complexes.

(A) DPVs obtained at the bare GC, N-CQDs/GC, β-CD-N-CQDs/GC, and WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrodes in 0.1MN2-saturated PBS (pH 7.0) with 20μM TNT and WP6-N-CQDs/GCE without TNT. (B) Effect of the solution pH on the reduction current density of 10.0μM TNT at the WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrode in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS. (C) DPVs obtained for different concentrations of TNT at the WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrode (0.0, 0.001μM, 0.005μM, 0.01μM, 0.05μM, 0.1μM, 0.3μM, 0.5μM, 1.0μM, 3.0μM, 5.0μM, 10.0μM, 15.0μM, and 20.0μM). (D) Plot of linear relationship between peak currents at −0.40V and TNT concentrations. (E) The chronoamperometric response obtained at the WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrode to the successive injection of 5μM TNT (a), NB (b), 2-NT (c), 4-NT (d), 2,4-DNT (e), and 2,6-DNT (f) at a constant potential of −0.40V. (F) The DPV responses of 5μM TNT on the WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrode at a potential of −0.40V in the absence (a) and presence of 5μM NB (b), 5μM 2-NT (c), 5μM 4-NT (d), 5μM 2,4-DNT (e), and 5μM 2,6-DNT (f).
Comparison of some electrode materials for electrochemical sensing of TNT.
Determination of TNT in tap water and lake water samples (n =6).

head.ce:title: Water-soluble pillar[6]arene functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with excellent supramolecular recognition capability and superior electrochemical sensing performance towards TNT

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Science and Technology, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Conservation and Utilization of Bio-Resources, Yunnan University, Kunming, 650091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Conservation and Utilization of Bio-Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive electrochemical sensing platform for TNT based on WP6-N-CQDs nanocomposite.

By combining the merits of water-soluble pillar[6]arenes (WP6s) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), a sensitive electrochemical sensing platform was constructed by using the WP6-N-CQD nanocomposites as an electrode material for the detection of trinitrotoluene (TNT). N-CQDs were prepared via microwave route by using urea and citric acid as raw materials. Two kinds of macrocyclic hosts, β-CD and WP6, were incorporated on to the N-CQDs via hydrogen bonding and π−π interactions, respectively. The FTIR, TGA, and XPS characterizitions demonstrated that the β-CD-N-CQD and WP6-N-CQD composites were successfully prepared. The WP6-N-CQD-modified glass carbon electrode (GCE) indicated an enhanced electrochemical signal than the β-CD-N-CQDs/GCE in the reduction of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), which was attributed to the higher supramolecular recognition capability of the WP6 host than that of β-CD towards TNT. The linear ranges of 0.001μM–1.0μM and 1.0μM–20.0μM were obtained by using this electrochemical sensing platform. The detection limit was 0.95nM (S/N=3). The developed electrochemical sensing technique was successfully applied for the detection of TNT in lake water samples. Therefore, WP6-N-CQD nanocomposites are promising for the construction of high performance electrochemical sensors for the detection of explosives.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decade, the utilization of artificial receptors, especially macrocyclic hosts, has garnered considerable attention in the field of optical/electrochemical sensing because of their high selectivity and sensitivity for the reliable, rapid and simple detection of different kinds of analytes [1]. Host-guest interactions have been widely used to construct nanoscale devices in electrochemical sensing or biosensing [2]. Recently, many researchers have attempted to develop macrocycle nanocomposites (e.g., cyclodextrin, calixarenes, pillararene) and carbon nanomaterials (e.g., graphene, carbon nanodots, CNT) for diversified applications, for instance, electrochemical/bio sensing, fluorescence sensing, cell/bio imaging, drug/gene transportation, and so on. For example, Guo et al. [3] demonstrated that cyclodextrin (CD)-graphene composites exhibited outstanding supramolecular recognition capabilities and enhanced electrochemical responses towards electroactive molecules than pure graphene. Zhu et al. [4] reported that β-CD-CNTs nanohybrids revealed higher electrochemical signals and excellent sensing performances than pure CNTs. Moreover, it has been proved that calix[6]arene-modified graphene can be employed as a ‘turn on’ receptor for fluorescence sensing L-carnitine in cells [5]. In addition, higher electrochemical responses were obtained on the calix[4,6,8]arene modified reduced graphene oxide (RGO) composites than pure RGO [6]. Yang et al. [7] also indicated that disulfide bridged β-CD-functionalized CNTs exhibited higher supramolecular recognition capabilities and higher electrochemical responses than that of natural β-CD-CNT composites towards three phenols.

As a new developing family of macrocycles, pillar[n]arenes, mainly pillar[5,6]arenes which were first reported by Ogoshi et al. [8]. Pillar[n]arenes are composed of hydroquinone units linked by methylene (–CH2–) bridges at their para-positions. They have a hydrophobic cavity sandwiched between two functionalizable rims and have shown excellent binding abilities towards various types of guests with high selectivities due to their rigid symmetrical pillar architectures and hydrophobic electron-donating cavities [9–15]. Besides, pillar[n]arenes also have several outstanding advantages such as easier synthesis, easier derivatization, and higher solubility in aqueous solutions as compared to other traditional macrocycles [16–18]. They have been described as “fascinating cyclophanes with a bright future” [19]. The synthesis, derivatization, host-guest chemistry, and self-assembly of pillar[n]arenes have been widely explored [20–24]. However, the electrochemical/optical sensing applications of pillar[n]arenes are rarely reported [25].

On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have emerged as a new family of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted considerable attention recently because of their distinct benefits, such as green synthesis, excellent solubility, easy functionalization, chemical inertness, stable photoluminescence, high electrochemical activity, and excellent biocompability [26–35]. As a green substitute for toxic transition metal quantum dots, CQDs have promising applications in many areas such as fluorescence/electrochemical sensing, electrocatalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery, etc. It has been repoted that N-doping can improve the electrocatalytic activity of carbon materials. For instance, N-doped carbon quantum dots [36], N-doped graphene quantum dots [37], and N-doped reduced graphene oxide [38] have exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity. Thus, N-CQDs were employed in the present study. If N-CQDs are functionalized with water-soluble pillar[n]arenes, new materials with the fascinating electrocatalytic property of N-CQDs and excellent supramolecular recognition and enrichment capability of pillar[n]arenes will likely be obtained. Therefore, the integration of N-CQDs and pillar[n]arenes could have numerous potential applications in electrochemical sensing.

TNT, or 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene, is a well-known explosive that is widely used in military and nonmilitary applications [36,37]. The detection of TNT remains an important area of research both for preventative measures as well as decontamination of affected sites [39]. In addition, TNT is toxic and mutagenic, and is known to contaminate soil and groundwater, increasing the need for sensitive and rapid detection methods. Recently, electrochemical sensing techniques have been widely used for the detection of TNT due to their inherent selectivity, ease of use, low cost, short analysis time, and low limit of detection [40]. Various electrode materials such as sliver NPs-graphene [41], ionic liquid-graphene [42], and Pt/Pd nanocube-graphene nanoribbons [43], as well as several metal oxide-based electrodes [44,45] have been employed for TNT detection. Nevertheless, there remains a need for electrode materials with unique properties that provide enhanced sensitivity and selectivity.

To the best of our knowledge, by combining the merits of water-soluble pillar[6]arene (WP6) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), a sensitive and selective electrochemical sensing platform based on WP6-N-CQDs composites modified electrode is reported here for the electrochemical reduction and determination of TNT for the first time. N-CQDs were prepared via microwave synthesis using citric acid and urea as raw materials. Two kinds of macrocycles, β-CD and WP6, were incorporated on to the N-CQDs via hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions, respectively. Due to the higher supramolecular recognition capability of WP6 than β-CD towards TNT, a higher electrochemical response was obtained at the WP6-N-CQD-modified electrode as compared to that of β-CD-N-CQDs towards TNT. The proposed electrochemical sensing platform is provided in Scheme 1 .

WP6 (Scheme S1) was synthesized according to a previously published procedure [46]. β-CD was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Lo., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). TNT, nitrobenzene (NB), 4-nitrotoluene (4-NT), 2-nitrotoluene (2-NT), 2,6-dinitrotoluene (2,6-DNT), and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT) were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (DW, 18MΩcm).

N-CQDs were prepared according to a previously published method with minor modifications [29]. Briefly, 3g urea and 3g citric acid were dissolved in 10mL DW. Then, the above mixed solution was heated in an 800W microwave oven for 4min, throughout this process, the mixed solution changed from a colorless liquid to a brown solid, suggesting the formation of N-CQDs. An aqueous solution of the N-CQDs was centrifuged (3000rpm, 10min) to remove the aggregated particles. The resulting brown supernatant was lyophilized to obtain the N-CQD powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of β-CD-N-CQDs and WP6-N-CQD composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fifty milliliter of 1.0mgmL−1 WP6 aqueous solution was prepared. One hundred milligram of N-CQDs was added into the above WP6 solution and sonicated at room temperature for 4h. Then, the resulting mixed suspension was centrifuged at 18,000rpm and washed using DW for three times to remove free WP6. Finally, the WP6-N-CQD composites were received by lyophilization. The β-CD-N-CQDs were obtained using a similar process except that the WP6 was replaced by β-CD.

A glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3mm in diameter) was polished with 0.05μm Al2O3 powder and sonicated in DW to remove the physically adsorbed Al2O3 and dried at room temperature under air. The WP6-N-CQDs were dissolved in DW at 1.0mgmL−1 with the aid of ultrasonic agitation for 30min, resulting in a homogeneous suspension. Using the same procedure, 1.0mgmL−1 homogeneous suspensions of β-CD-N-CQDs and N-CQDs were also prepared. The WP6-N-CQD-modified GC electrode was assembled by casting 5μL of 1.0mgmL−1 WP6-N-CQD suspension onto the GC electrode. The prepared working electrode was dried at room temperature and termed WP6-N-CQDs/GCE. The β-CD-N-CQDs/GC and N-CQDs/GC electrodes were also constructed by using the similar procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of β-CD-N-CQD and WP6-N-CQD composites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the N-CQDs was determined using transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the N-CQDs were monodispersed with an average size of approximately 1.6±0.3nm (Fig. 1A, inset), which was determined using the size distribution by measuring approximately 100 carbon dots. The TEM images of the β-CD-N-CQDs and WP6-N-CQDs were also obtained (Fig. S1). It can ben seen that the TEM images of the β-CD-N-CQDs and WP6-N-CQDs show some differences compared with the pure N-CQDs (Fig. 1A) and the size (approximately 2.0nm) is a little bigger than that of N-CQDs (1.6±0.3nm). The Raman spectrum of the N-CQDs (Fig. 1B) showed a peak at approximately 1360cm−1 caused by the D (sp3-hybridized) band, which is associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite. And another band at 1598cm−1 is the G (sp2-hybridized) band, which is ascribed to the E2g mode of graphite and is related to the vibrations of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [29]. A relative intensity of the disordered D band and crystalline G band (ID/IG) of 1.27 was obtained for the C-dots. As illustrated in Fig. 1C, the X-ray diffractometry (XRD) pattern of the N-CQDs contained one broad peak at 2θ=26°, which was similar to the graphite lattice spacing, ascribed to the highly disordered carbon atoms. All these results including the presence of the D band and G band in Raman spectrum, and the broad diffraction peak at 2θ=26° in the XRD pattern demonstrated that the N-CQDs had a similar structure to graphene. This result was in accordance with previous reports [30,47].

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of N-CQDs, β-CD and β-CD-N-CQDs were obtained, and are shown in Fig. 1D. The FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs contained broad absorption bands at 3100–3500cm−1, which were caused by the stretching vibrations of NH and OH. The absorption bands at 1350–1460cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CH2 and the bands at 1610–1710cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibrations of CO [29]. Compared to that of the N-CQDs, the FTIR spectrum of the β-CD-N-CQDs contained representative CD absorption bands of the coupled OH bending vibrations/COC stretching at 1152cm−1, CH/OH bending vibrations at 1410cm−1, coupled CC/CO stretching/OH bending vibrations at 1029cm−1, and CH2 stretching vibrations at 2925cm−1, demonstrating that the β-CD molecules were grafted on the N-CQDs [3,7]. The FTIR spectra of N-CQDs, WP6, and WP6-N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1E. The FTIR spectrum of WP6-N-CQDs showed absorption bands at 1490cm−1, 2950cm−1, and 3013cm−1, which were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the aromatic skeleton, CH3, and aromatic CH, respectively [15]. These results demonstrated that WP6 was successfully assembled on the N-CQDs.

Considering the molecular structure of β-CD and WP6, the self-assembly interaction mechanism of β-CD and WP6 on the N-CQDs was facilitated by hydrogen bond and π−π interactions, respectively. Since β-CDs and N-CQDs contain abundant OH groups, strong intermolecular hydrogen bond interactions are easy to occur. Meanwhile, π−π noncovalent interactions between the benzene rings of calix[n]arenes and the rGO surface were illuminated previously [15]. We therefore deduced that π−π interactions must exist between the benzene rings of WP6 and N-CQDs, because the molecular structures of pillar[n]arenes are similar to those of calix[n]arenes.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to evaluate the amount of β-CD or WP6 on the N-CQDs, as exhibited in Fig. 1F. The N-CQDs revealed an abrupt mass loss at approximately 200°C, which was attributed to the pyrolysis of the oxygen groups. The loss in mass reached about 58.0wt% at approximately 600°C. The β-CD-N-CQDs exhibited an abrupt mass loss at approximately 260°C, which was caused by the decomposition of β-CD [4,48]. When the temperature was 600°C, the mass loss reached about 75.0wt%. The β-CD that grafted on N-CQDs was approximately 17.0wt% based on a rough estimate. When the temperature was about 300°C, the WP6-N-CQDs demonstrated a mass loss due to the pyrolysis of WP6 [15]; the loss of WP6-N-CQDs reached about 80.0wt% at a temperature of 600°C. The mass loss caused by the pyrolysis of WP6 was estimated to be 22.0wt% by deducting the loss of the N-CQDs,

The surface compositions and elemental analysis of the N-CQDs, β-CD-N-CQDs, and WP6-N-CQDs were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Three peaks at approximately 284.6, 399.9, and 530.7eV were present in the XPS survey spectra, as shown in Fig. 1G; they corresponded to the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s core levels, respectively. The XPS spectrum indicated that the atomic percentages of C, O, and N in N-CQDs were 51.53%, 24.58%, and 23.89%, respectively. Similarly, the atomic percentages of C, O, and N in β-CD-N-CQDs were 56.57%, 34.90%, and 8.53%, respectively. The WP6-N-CQDs were composed of 52.45% C, 26.49% O, and 21.06% N. The C 1s XPS spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 2 A) illustrated four components corresponded to OCO at ca. 288.60eV, CO at ca. 287.80eV, CN at ca. 285.40eV, and CC at ca. 284.60eV [28,32,35]. The deconvoluted N 1s XPS spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 2B) at 400.70eV, 399.90eV, and 399.30eV corresponded to the nitrogen atom of NH, pyrrolic-like N, and pyridinic-like N groups, respectively [31,33,34]. The O 1s XPS spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 2C) could be deconvoluted into peaks at ca. 533.40eV, ca. 532.00eV, ca. 531.40eV, and ca. 530.60eV, suggesting the exist of OCO*, CO, *OCO, and OH [31]. The C 1s XPS spectrum of β-CD-N-CQDs (Fig. 2D) was divided into five peaks at ca. 284.60eV, ca. 285.40eV, ca. 286.50eV, ca. 287.80eV, and ca. 288.80eV. They could be ascribed to the CC, CN, CO, CO, and OCO groups, respectively. The three deconvoluted peaks at ca. 399.30eV, ca. 400.00eV, and ca. 400.70eV in the N 1s spectrum of β-CD-N-CQDs (Fig. 2E) were caused by the pyridinic-like N, pyrrolic-like N, and NH groups, respectively. The four deconvoluted peaks in the O 1s spectrum of β-CD-N-CQDs (Fig. 2F) at 530.80eV, 531.50eV, 532.50eV, and 533.40eV could be attributed to the OH, *OCO, CO, and OCO* groups, respectively. The C 1s XPS spectrum of WP6-N-CQDs (Fig. 2G) could be resolved into five peaks with binding energies of ca. 284.60eV, ca. 285.40eV, ca. 286.30eV, ca. 287.90eV, and ca. 288.70eV, corresponding to the CC, CN, CO, CO, and OCO groups, respectively. Deconvolution of the N 1s spectrum of WP6-N-CQDs (Fig. 2H) showed three peaks at 399.40eV, 400.00eV, and 400.60eV. They were ascribed to pyridinic-like N, pyrrolic-like N, and NH groups, respectively. Four deconvoluted peaks were observed in the O 1s spectrum of WP6-N-CQDs (Fig. 2I) at 530.70eV, 531.40eV, 532.00eV, and 533.40eV due to the OH, *OCO, CO, and OCO* groups, respectively. Although the β-CD and WP6 molecules can not be distinguished from TEM images, they were proved by the FTIR, TGA, and XPS characterizations, indicating the successful assembly of the β-CD-N-CQDs and WP6-N-CQDs composites.

The β-CD/TNT and WP6/TNT inclusion complexes were studied by performing the molecular docking [49]. DOCK6 program was used to simulate the binding models of β-CD/TNT and WP6/TNT. Fig. 3 A and B illustrated the docked conformation of the β-CD/TNT complex based on its lowest energy, which revealed that TNT was included in the β-CD cavity from the narrow opening. The results indicated that strong hydrophobic interactions formed between β–CD and TNT caused by the high van der Waals contribution of −15.97kcalmol−1. In addition, O1, O2, and O4 atoms on the NO2 groups of TNT formed hydrogen bonds with the −OH groups of β–CD with bond lengths of 3.2, 3.1, and 3.3Å, respectively. The electrostatic interaction contribution was −1.92kcalmol−1. The lowest energy docked conformation of the WP6/TNT complex is illustrated in Fig. 3C and D. The TNT molecule was included in the cavity of WP6. The benzene ring of TNT formed face-to-face π–π interactions with the benzene moiety of WP6 and T shaped π–π interactions with the opposite benzene moiety. The electrostatic interaction contribution was −5.85kcalmol−1. In addition, hydrophobic interactions led to a van der Waals contribution of −14.16kcalmol−1. The docking scores of β-CD/TNT and WP6/TNT were also studied. As revealed in Table S1, the lowest energy score of β-CD/TNT was much lower than that of WP6/TNT, implying that TNT/WP6 inclusion complex was more stable than TNT/β-CD.

Fig. S2 illustrates the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of bare GC, N-CQDs/GC, β-CD-N-CQDs/GC, and WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrodes. Compared with the bare GCE, the charge transfer resistance (R ct) of the N-CQDs/GC electrode was much smaller, implying that the N-CQD film can significantly accelerate the interfacial charge transfer. In the case of β-CD-N-CQDs/GC and WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrodes, the semicircles increased to approximately 1000 and 1150Ω, respectively. This was because that the non-conductive β-CD or WP6 can hinder electron transfer and make the interfacial charge transfer more difficult. The β-CD and WP6 were successfully grafted on the N-CQDs, as demonstrated by FTIR and TGA. The increased R ct is not direct proof that β-CD or WP6 were grafted on the N-CQDs, but can serve as auxiliary evidence [50–52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical reduction of TNT on the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical detection methods have broad applications in small molecule sensing because of their advantages including reasonable sensitivity, rapid response, low cost, simple procedure, and ease of miniaturization. Different kinds of carbon materials, such as graphene nanosheets, carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanohorns [36], have exhibited satisfactory electrochemical activities and are regarded as ideal electrode materials. N-CQDs, a new carbon-based material, also possess favorable merits in electrochemical applications, which have not drawn enough attention to date. Herein, by combing the merits of N-CQDs and macrocycles, N-CQDs/GC, β-CD-N-CQDs/GC, and WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrodes were constructed for the electrochemical reduction of TNT. The DPVs for the reduction of 20.0μM TNT at bare GCE, N-CQDS/GCE, β-CD-N-CQDs/GCE, and WP6-N-CQDs/GCE were studied in 0.1MN2-saturated PBS (pH 7.0). As demonstrated in Fig. 4 A, there were three well-defined reduction peaks located at approximately −0.40, −0.55, and −0.70V, which were caused by the stepwise reduction of different NO2 groups of TNT to the corresponding hydroxylamine and amine [53–55]. However, when the WP6-N-GQDs/GCE was not loaded with TNT by immersion in PBS, no reduction peaks were appeared in the DPV curve, indicating that the three reduction peaks were indeed from the reduction of the −NO2 group in TNT. The reduction current recorded at the N-CQDs/GCE was much higher than that at the bare GCE, indicating that there was much higher electrocatalytic activity on the N-GQDs [36]. It was assumed that the N-doping and small quantum dot size contributed to the excellent electrocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs. The energy gap of the HOMO−LUMO (H−L gap) of the N-GQDs can be decreased by nitrogen doping, which can in turn enhance the electron transfer and increase the TNT reduction rate [37]. A control experiment was studied by using CQDs without doping synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid [26,27]. As shown in Fig. S3, the signal amplification is very weak by using the CQDs without doping, indicating that the N-doping can effectively improve the electrocatalytic activity towrds TNT reduction. Notably, the reduction current of TNT at β-CD-N-CQDs/GCE was enhanced significantly as compared to that at the N-CQD-modified electrode, which could be caused by the high supramolecular recognition capability of β-CD that formed inclusion complex with TNT, therefore, resulting in the enrichment of large amount of TNT molecules on the electrode surface. Interestingly, the reduction peak current of TNT at WP6-N-CQDs/GCE was remarkably higher than that at β-CD-N-CQDs/GCE. The results indicated that the WP6 has a higher supramolecular recognition capability than β-CD towards TNT. From the results of molecular docking study, the lowest energy score of β-CD/TNT was much lower than that of WP6/TNT, implying that TNT/WP6 inclusion complex was more stable than TNT/β-CD. Thus, more amount of TNT molecules could be included in the WP6 than that of β-CD, which resulted the higher electrochemical signal at the WP6-N-CQDs/GC than that at the β-CD-N-CQDs/GC electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the electrochemical sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of TNT at the WP6-N-GQDs/GCE could be influenced by accumulation time and solution pH. Therefore, the accumulation time and solution pH conditions were optimized. The accumulation time of TNT on the WP6-N-GQDs/GCE at 0V vs SCE increased until the electrochemical reduction current was maximal (Fig. S4). The peak current for the reduction of TNT increased with the increasing of the accumulation time. The peak current reached a maximum at an accumulation time of 200s. Thus, the optimal accumulation time for detection of TNT was selected as 200s. The solution pH was also optimized (Fig. 4B). In the pH range from 5.0 to 9.0, an optimal current was found at approximately pH 7.0. According to the electrochemical reaction mechanism for the reduction of nitro groups (Scheme S2), a weak acid buffer solution is favourable. The electrochemical signal shows a maximum at pH 7.0. This may be due to the fact that the TNT guest molecules are included in the WP6 host in the present system. Accordingly, pH 7.0 PBS was used for reduction of TNT. The effect of the concentration of WP6-N-CQDs suspension on the electrochemical signal of 5.0μM TNT at the WP6-N-CQDs/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS was provided in Fig. S5. We can seen that the electrochemical signal increases not significantly when the WP6-N-CQDs concentration is greater than 1.0mgmL−1. Thus, 1.0mgmL−1 WP6-N-CQDs suspension was selected.

Next, the electrochemical selectivity of the detection system was investigated. The current responses to a series of nitroaromatic compounds including NB, 4-NT, 2-NT, 2,6-DNT, and 2,4-DNT were investigated on WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrode. The chronoamperometric curve obtained at −0.40V (Fig. 4E) revealed that the addition of those interfering substances did not impact the detection of TNT, demonstrating its excellent capability to distinguish TNT from other substances. DPV was also used to study the selectivity. As shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. 4F, the results suggested that the presence of 5μM NB, 5μM 2-NT, 5μM 4-NT, 5μM 2,4-DNT, and 5μM 2,6-DNT did not affect the DPV response of 5μM TNT on the WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrode at a potential of −0.40V. To estimate the practical applications of WP6-N-CQDs/GCE in real sample analysis, ultrapure water was replaced with tap and lake water in the buffer solution. As shown in Table S2, the recovery in six parallel measurements ranged from 92.5 to 104.0%, indicating the promising applications of the WP6-N-CQD-based detection platform for real sample determination.

The electrochemical signals of 10μM of TNT using six equal WP6-N-CQDs/GC electrodes were compared to evaluate the repeatability of the sensor. The six sensors exhibited similar electrochemical signals with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.2%, indicating a satisfactory repeatability. A long-term storage assay was used to examine the stability of the sensor. The constructed sensor was stored in a refrigerator at 4°C when not in use. The electrochemical signals for reduction TNT were measured every 5days. Initial responses of over 94.3% and 88.2% remained after storage for 5 and 10days, respectively, indicating an acceptable stability of the sensor.

In summary, the β-CD/TNT and WP6/TNT inclusion complexes were investigated by molecular docking, and a stronger molecular recognition capability of WP6 than β-CD towards TNT was demonstrated. The β-CD-N-CQD and WP6-N-CQD nanocomposites were obtained by incorporating free β-CD and WP6 onto the N-CQDs, which were prepared by microwave synthesis, via hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions, respectively. The FTIR, TGA, and XPS characterizations proved that the macrocycles were effectively grafted on the N-CQDs. Electrochemical results revealed that the WP6-N-CQDs exhibited a higher supramolecular recognition and enrichment capabilities and showed a higher peak current towards TNT than β-CD-N-CQDs. A sensitive and selective electrochemical sensing platform was constructed by integrating the merits of WP6 and N-CQDs. Because of the synergistic effects of N-CQDs and WP6, the WP6-N-CQD-modified electrode exhibited a linear response ranging from 0.001μM to 1μM and form 1μM to 20μM with a LOD of 0.95nM for TNT. These results hint that WP6-N-CQD composites are perfect materials for electrochemical sensing platforms.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under the Grant No. 51361028, 51161025, 51661033, and 31660538.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.10.185.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xin Ran is currently pursuing her Doctor's degree in Yunnan University, China. Her research interests are mainly focused on functional hybrid materials for electrocatalysis, electrochemical/bio sensing, energy conversion, etc.

Qing Qu received his Ph.D. degree from Institute of Metal Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China, in 2002. Since then he has been working at Yunnan University as a professor. His current research interests are advanced functional materials for electrochemical sensors and biosensors.
Xingcan Qian is currently pursuing her Master's degree in Yunnan University, China. Her research interests are focused on electrochemical sensors and biosensors.
Wei Xie is currently pursuing his Master's degree in Yunnan University, China. His research interests are mainly focused on theoretical and computational chemistry.
Shunling Li is currently pursuing her Master's degree in Yunnan University, China. Her research interests are mainly focused on electrochemical sensors and microbial corrosion.
Lei Li received her Master's degree in Yunnan University, China, in 1997. Since then she has been working at Yunnan University. Her current research interests are biosensor and microbial metabolism.

Long Yang received his Ph.D. degree in physical chemistry from Yunnan University, China, in 2017. He is currently working at Southwest Forestry University. His research interests are mainly focused on nanomaterials for optical/electrochemical sensing and catalysis.
